title: Binary growth check, part 1 of 2
body:
Add a CI check to prevent unwanted bloat in binary images,
by building a baseline (pre-PR) binary then comparing file
sizes post-PR.

We piggyback onto the existing 'Build Each Commit' CI check
because it gives us an easy way to run 'make' against the
parent commit.

This is part 1 of 2: adding the script, not the Makefile rule.
We can't add the Makefile rule now because the script it would
invoke does not exist in the parent commit.

Signed-off-by: Ed Santiago <santiago@redhat.com>

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *edsantiago* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)~~ [edsantiago] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

In a nutshell: once part 2 merges, this will fail CI if podman or podman-remote or rootlessport grow by more than **100k** (or whatever value is desired).

If someone has a better idea on where to fit this in (instead of `Build Each Commit`), I'll be glad to hear it. Likewise if I've missed some important concern with the leave-tree-in-funky-state issue.

edit: MAX_BIN_GROWTH=10
@edsantiago thanks for taking a lead on this!  awesome start.
btw to fix the windows cross build test you need to rebase: https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/13514/commits/33aa2f2d1c8476a973d83dfefacbb60a36d8d4b1

I pushed an experimental version that uses the following logic:
* Query github for comments on the PR
* Look for comments made by users with `MEMBER` status, containing the string `MAX_BIN_GROWTH=nnn`.

Good news: it seemed to work, suggesting that we can use the Cirrus token for queries¹.

Bad news: the `authorAssociation` query field seems to be unreliable. When I query from my laptop, I get myself and baude listed as `MEMBER`. When querying from this script in CI, or from the [github explorer using my personal login](https://docs.github.com/en/graphql/overview/explorer), we're both `COLLABORATOR`. Therefore this is not a mechanism we can trust.

Semi-good news: it should be possible to query on labels, so we could add a label such as `growingpains` or `bloat_ok` and pass if that label is set. I don't like that as much as explicitly giving a numeric value in the github history, but shrug.

Anyhow, that's it for me today. I'm on PTO tomorrow (Wednesday March 16).

 [1] although it's possible that the query succeeded _despite_ the token, not because of it; i.e., github treated it as an anonymous query. It might be possible to look into this further by logging the http headers, but that's an exercise for later.

The comment-based approach is impossible: `authorAssociation` is broken in github, completely nondeterministic. Proof:
* [log 1, from last week](https://api.cirrus-ci.com/v1/task/6452326536839168/logs/main.log) shows baude as COLLABORATOR, Luap99 as MEMBER
*  [log 2, from today](https://api.cirrus-ci.com/v1/task/4643962056081408/logs/main.log) shows the opposite.

Unless/until that is fixed, the only approach seems to be checking for a label. Let's decide on one, please:
* `bloat_ok`
* `bloat_approved`
* `growingpains`
* `size100k` (possibly with a supplemental `size500k` label and check, to add an extra check on super-duper-huge PRs)

@containers/podman-maintainers please add suggestions as desired, then agree on one (or two, if the 100/500k one).
bloat approved sgtm, and I'm at 50k for size

`bloat_approved` going once, going twice...

...but sizewise, I'm still nervous about a one-size-fits-all. A single tag can be used to allow any amount of growth, whether 50k or 5M. Any ideas on how to make this safer?
@edsantiago lets see how 50k goes.  it is binary size, so that is quite a bit?

Because we're talking about timings:

| type | distro       | user     |    local |   remote | container |
| ---- | --------     | ----     |    ----: |   -----: | --------: |
| int  | fedora-35    | root     |    37:13 |    52:36 |     33:14 |
| int  | ubuntu-2110  | root     |    30:22 |    35:15 |
| int  | fedora-35    | rootless |    37:54 |          |
| int  | ubuntu-2110  | rootless |    34:35 |          |
| sys  | fedora-35    | root     |    27:26 |    18:07 |
| sys  | ubuntu-2110  | root     |    25:30 |    14:28 |
| sys  | fedora-35    | rootless |    26:23 |    29:01 |
| sys  | ubuntu-2110  | rootless |    24:40 |          |
| bud  | fedora-35    | root     |    21:07 |    20:28 |

Maybe I'm reading it wrong, but it looks like this will only check if the size grows unacceptably WITHIN the context of a PR.  I think instead you want the baseline to be one (merged) commit higher.  Probably `$CIRRUS_LAST_GREEN_CHANGE` would be a good starting place. ***Note:*** Running this check in CI (with that variable) for a new tag will likely fail - so task needs `skip: $CIRRUS_TAG != ''`).  Could also use `$CIRRUS_BASE_SHA`, but that will only work on PRs.

Ref: https://cirrus-ci.org/guide/writing-tasks/#environment-variables

Non-blocking: I think perhaps a simpler (and therefore more reliable & easy to maintain) strategy for this check is to just [download the latest binary](https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/13559), and compare the size vs `bin/whatever` in a dedicated (new) cirrus-CI task.  No compilation is needed, that's already done for you in the `Build for XYZ` tasks and restored in later tasks by `gopath_cache`.  This way also preserves a "separation-of-concerns" WRT the build-each-commit task - intended to confirm commit-order sanity and therefore `revert` capability.  Conflating two separate goals in that task may lead to unintended consequences later on (IMHO).

> if I've missed some important concern with the leave-tree-in-funky-state issue.

This is fine...but does beg the question: Should it be in the `Makefile` at all then?  Maybe just do the whole thing from `runner.sh` to keep developers from accidentally doing something only-ever intended for CI.
Good idea, and it's easy. I will do so in part 2.

UPDATE: "good idea" refers to doing this in runner.sh. I am not going to do the download-binaries thing for many many reasons, most important of which is, that does not do what we desire (check the growth within a PR). Using prebuilt binaries would check against completely arbitrary, random image sizes. (Reason: prebuilt binaries bear no relation whatsoever to a given PR)

Fair enough.  My concern for **only** checking the size within a PR (and therefore the suggestion for using `$CIRRUS_LAST_GREEN_CHANGE` or `$CIRRUS_BASE_SHA` as a base) is this scenario:

1. First commit in a PR grows binary by 500MB
2. Check confirms binary hasn't grown (500 == 500).
3. PR is merged, and binary grows by 500MB

Same concern applies in the context of CI running on a tag, where there isn't anything to rebase, HEAD is disjointed from any branch or other commits.  Though I could easily be not understanding how `make build-all-new-commits GIT_BASE_BRANCH=origin/$DEST_BRANCH` works.  I still think the task or script does need to be bypassed when `$CIRRUS_TAG` is non-empty.

>First commit in a PR grows binary by 500MB

This tells me that I haven't explained myself well enough. I will try to do better in part 2.
